turns.content: Imagine I'm trying to capture a TCP connection on my laptop, but whenever I try to read the socket, it returns garbage because I forgot to handle something fundamental about how the network layer works.

That sounds like you're missing the distinction between your application data and the actual network frames. When you use raw sockets for TCP, you aren't getting just the payload; you are receiving a full Ethernet frame which contains an EtherType field that tells you whether it's actually IP or ARP traffic.
So I need to manually parse those frames? But what exactly should I be looking at in the buffer before I even consider the payload?

You absolutely do. With pnet, your socket will return a raw byte stream containing an Ethernet header followed by either ARP or IP headers depending on the EtherType value. You must first check that field to know which Rust type you are expected to deserialize next.
This is getting confusing because I don't understand why my application code doesn't care about these headers when building a simple TCP client, yet here they block me from reading the actual data.

Think of it like this: your application logic only cares about the message body, but the network stack presents everything as a single envelope. You have to open that envelope first using pnet's traits and types before you can extract the IP packet or TCP segment that your app actually uses.
Wait, so if I use pnet::Packet instead of a raw Vec<u8>, does it automatically handle this parsing for me?

Exactly. By wrapping the raw bytes in a pnet::Packet and then calling methods like Packet::as_ethernet(), you get an automatic iterator that yields only the relevant frame types, skipping over the useless ARP or IP headers until you reach the TCP segment.
